ops.body:
# The soundness caveat (both languages)

LOCAL_PEERTOKEN resolves the peer socket's process at QUERY time — XNU reads it from the socket's last_pid at getsockopt time (bsd/kern/uipc_usrreq.c), unlike LOCAL_PEERCRED which is connect-frozen. It therefore does NOT bind identity to the connector: a same-UID peer can fork a holder and exec a genuine signed binary to lend its identity, and the UID floor does not confine fd delegation across a setuid/SCM_RIGHTS boundary. This is a **known-unsound binding for code authentication**. A surface needing a real per-message identity guarantee uses XPC (setCodeSigningRequirement), not this Policy; that hardening is a later phase.

`trust/policy.go` `Check` and `Sources/DaemonKit/PeerTrust.swift` are semantic twins: same floor, same requirement semantics, same caveat. Edit them in lockstep.

# The check ladder (fail-closed everywhere)

1. Same-effective-UID floor, unconditional on every platform — no configuration disables or replaces it; a Trust callback (wire.Server) augments it, never bypasses it.
2. With a Requirement configured: resolve the peer's SecCode from its raw 32-byte audit_token_t (a raw getsockopt read — GetsockoptString truncates at the first NUL, and the token's pid/uid words routinely hold NULs), then SecCodeCheckValidity against the compiled designated requirement.
3. A configured Requirement with no verifier is ErrNoVerifier — a fail-closed denial, never a downgrade to UID-only. A **nil Requirement is explicit UID-only trust — the floor alone — and succeeds on ANY build, ordinary signed darwin builds included**; UID-only is a configuration choice, never a fallback. The daemonkit_unsigned build tag (local-test builds; release CI must reject it) drops the verifier entirely, so a *configured* Requirement is denied there too.

# Requirement semantics

Both TeamID and Identifier are mandatory: TeamID-only grants every team binary authority over the socket; identifier-only is not globally unique across teams. DRString renders the Developer ID anchor (OIDs 1.2.840.113635.100.6.2.6 = Developer ID CA, 1.2.840.113635.100.6.1.13 = Developer ID Application leaf — together "Developer ID", excluding Mac App Store and development signatures) pinned to the quoted TeamID and identifier — never a cdhash (pins one build) or a TeamID-only clause (same-team lateral authority).

# The hardened-runtime gate (requireHardenedRuntime)

Unless AllowUnhardened: CS_RUNTIME required; CS_GET_TASK_ALLOW and CS_DEBUGGED rejected; library validation must hold via CS_REQUIRE_LV/CS_FORCED_LV or, failing those, an entitlements dictionary proven free of every injection entitlement (get-task-allow, debugger, allow-jit, allow-unsigned-executable-memory, disable-library-validation — the last inert when the kernel enforces LV anyway). Entitlements not in dictionary form are rejected: they cannot be proven clean. cs-bit values are verified against xnu's cs_blobs.h. AllowUnhardened is the ONE bypass — relaxes only the runtime/LV/injection gate, never the DR or the UID floor — for a consumer shipping an audited third-party dylib it cannot library-validate (fusekit-holder with libfuse-t). Enabling it is a security decision.

# Pinned limitations (trust/limitation_darwin_test.go)

The residual-limitation taxonomy is pinned in-tree. **TF1 (fork/exec substitution)** has two pins: a peer that execs /bin/sleep after connecting authenticates as /bin/sleep (query-time image), and Policy.Check ACCEPTS a peer that execs a Developer ID fixture satisfying the exact policy after connecting — the documented bypass. **TF2 (PID reuse)** — a recycled pid lets a later LOCAL_PEERTOKEN read resolve an unrelated image — is documented via a skipped test (nondeterministic to pin in-tree). **TF5 (fd delegation)** — SCM_RIGHTS passing or a setuid binary detaches identity from the connection's actor — is documented via a skipped test (needs a privileged multi-process harness, and connection admission cannot attribute later delegation). Helper choreography: the re-exec helper execs a FOREIGN image, never os.Executable() (no fork-bomb risk); it holds a non-CLOEXEC dup(2) of the socket fd so the peer end survives the exec and last_pid still resolves the same pid; a go-ahead byte serializes the baseline assertion (untrusted test binary) before the substitution flips the query-time identity.

# Swift-side CF quirks (PeerTrust.swift)

- SecCodeCopySigningInformation needs the audit-token guest; Swift imports SecCode/SecStaticCode as unrelated types → bit cast.
- Entitlement cleanliness: only both keys ABSENT is clean; a present-but-undecodable value cannot be proven clean. Only a genuine CFBoolean false is clean — CFEqual alone also matches CFNumber(0) through NSNumber bridging (Go compares the kCFBooleanFalse singleton by identity).
